section: Title, Authors, Abstract, and Introduction Part 1
text: Spatial integration of multi-omics single-cell data with SIMO. Penghui Yang, Kaiyu Jin, Yue Yao, Lijun Jin, Xin Shao, Chengyu Li, Xiaoyan Lu & Xiaohui Fan. Published in Nature Communications (2025) 16:1265. Abstract: Technical limitations in spatial and single-cell omics sequencing pose challenges for capturing multimodal information at the spatial scale. To address this, we develop SIMO, a computational method for the Spatial Integration of Multi-Omics datasets through probabilistic alignment. Unlike previous tools, SIMO expands beyond integrating spatial transcriptomics with scRNA-seq, enabling integration across multiple single-cell modalities such as chromatin accessibility and DNA methylation, which have not been co-profiled spatially before. We benchmark SIMO on simulated datasets, demonstrating its high accuracy and robustness. Application on biological datasets reveals its ability to detect topological patterns of cells and their regulatory modes. Analysis of real-world data uncovers multimodal spatial heterogeneity, offering deeper insights into spatial organization and regulation. Introduction: The evolution of spatial omics and single-cell sequencing technologies has transformed our ability to study tissues. However, current spatial omics primarily focuses on a single modality (transcriptome/proteome), making it challenging to characterize multiple modalities on the same section with cellular resolution. Conversely, single-cell omics provides detailed snapshots across modalities (gene expression, chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation) but loses spatial information during tissue dissociation, which is crucial for understanding cell states and microenvironments.

section: Introduction Part 2 and Overview of SIMO
text: Current computational methods can integrate ST with scRNA-seq or map paired single-cell multi-omics data. However, they either focus solely on transcriptomics, rely on paired data, or fail to effectively incorporate spatial information. A significant gap remains in tools that can map diverse single-cell data across multiple modalities within a spatial context. To address this, we introduce SIMO, a computational tool for integrating spatial transcriptomics with multiple non-spatial single-cell omics data (RNA, ATAC, DNA methylation). SIMO performs precise spatial mapping, constructs spatial patterns of cell clusters, and enables in-depth analysis of gene regulatory networks in the spatial dimension. Overview of SIMO: SIMO is designed for spatial mapping and integration of single-cell data from various modalities. It addresses the challenge by breaking down the task into a sequential mapping process for each modality. Initially, SIMO integrates ST data with transcriptomics data, as they originate from the same modality, to minimize interference from modal differences. This step uses the computational strategy of the previously developed tool SpaTrio: it uses k-NN to construct a spatial graph and a modality map, and uses fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport to calculate the mapping between cells and spots, retaining the hyperparameter α for balancing transcriptomic differences and graph distances. Cell coordinates are fine-tuned based on transcriptome similarity.

section: SIMO Methodology for Modality 2 and Downstream Analysis
text: Next, SIMO integrates non-transcriptomic single-cell data (e.g., scATAC-seq) through a new sequential mapping process. It preprocesses both mapped scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq data, obtaining initial clusters. Gene activity scores (calculated from chromatin accessibility) serve as a key linkage point. SIMO calculates average Pearson Correlation Coefficients (PCCs) of gene activity scores between cell groups, facilitating label transfer between modalities using an Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) algorithm. For cell groups with identical labels, SIMO constructs modality-specific k-NN graphs and calculates distance matrices. Cell alignment probabilities are determined via Gromov-Wasserstein (GW) transport. Finally, SIMO allocates scATAC-seq data to specific spatial locations and adjusts cell coordinates based on modality similarity. By modifying the UOT cost matrix, SIMO achieves spatial mapping of various omics types, regardless of their positive or negative biological relationship with the transcriptome. Downstream Analysis: SIMO's capabilities include gene regulation analysis and spatial regulation analysis. Gene regulation analysis transforms data into matrices with gene names as features (e.g., motif activity matrices from ATAC). It calculates PCCs between fold changes in motif activity and gene expression to analyze regulatory patterns. Spatial regulation analysis integrates data from both modalities and their spatial information. It applies spatial smoothing and cross-modal smoothing, calculates a regulatory score as the ratio of feature pairs, constructs a spatial kernel matrix, and identifies feature modules with similar spatial regulation patterns through weighted correlation analysis and Consensus Clustering (CC).

section: Evaluation on Simulated Datasets and Comparison with Tools
text: Evaluation on simulated datasets: We used mouse cerebral cortex SNARE-seq and ISSAAC-seq data to construct simulated spatial datasets with varying complexity. ST data simulation involved sample extraction, merging, coordinate allocation, and introducing noise via a pseudocount δ. Evaluation metrics: cell mapping accuracy, Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of deconvoluted cell type proportions, and Jensen-Shannon Distance (JSD) for 'spot' and 'type'. In simpler spatial patterns, SIMO (α=0.1) demonstrated greater stability as δ increased compared to using only gene expression (α=0) or only graph data (α=1). Under high noise (δ=5), SIMO (α=0.1) accurately recovered >91% of cells in Pattern 1 and >88% in Pattern 2. In complex patterns (Patterns 3-6, 10 cell types), SIMO showed exceptional stability. Setting α=0.1 generally yielded the best performance. Comparison with existing tools: We compared SIMO with CARD, Tangram, Seurat, LIGER, and Scanorama on simulated and three biological datasets. Under noise-free conditions, SIMO significantly outperformed others across all simulated datasets, achieving the lowest RMSE and JSD values. On biological datasets, SIMO displayed superior performance, leading other methods except for a slight underperformance in one dataset compared to Tangram. When noise was introduced, SIMO's advantages became even more apparent, achieving the best results across all metrics. SIMO's mapping results across each modality were stable, excelling in the second modality (epigenetic data) over other tools. It was capable of accurately merging transcriptomic and epigenetic data even when signals showed opposite correlations (e.g., H3K27me3 data).

section: Evaluation of Biological Data and Application to Mouse Cortex
text: Evaluation of biological data: We tested SIMO on three real spatial multi-omics datasets: 1) Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq from mouse embryos, 2) Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq from mouse brain, 3) Spatial CUT&Tag-RNA-seq (H3K27me3) from mouse brain. SIMO accurately reconstructed spatial distribution of multi-omics features, preserving biological relationships. In Dataset 1 (mouse embryo), analysis of radial glia and neurons across three regions revealed distinct spatial omics regulation patterns. SIMO accurately reconstructed these patterns. In Dataset 3, SIMO effectively captured the anti-correlation between H3K27me3 and RNA, reconstructing 611 feature associations, the highest among compared methods. Application to the mouse cortex: We applied SIMO to a mouse brain multimodal dataset (gene expression, DNA methylation, 10x Visium ST). SIMO accurately reconstructed the stratified cortical layers (L2/3, L4, L5, L6). The spatial distribution of DNA methylation data also reflected layer stratification. SIMO demonstrated significant advantage in spatial mapping of DNA methylation signals compared to existing tools. Integrated analysis revealed heterogeneity within the Layer5a cell type, showing it aligned with mL4 and mDL-2 DNA methylation types. Unsupervised clustering uncovered intrinsic heterogeneity within Layer5a. Expression patterns of key markers (Cux2, Rorb, etc.) varied across modalities and showed distinctive regional distribution.

section: Multi-omics Regulation in Mouse Cortex and Application to Myocardial Infarction Part 1
text: We combined gene expression and DNA methylation data to explore gene regulation. Population-level PCCs showed most gene-methylation pairs had negative correlations, consistent with DNA methylation's repressive role. For the Layer5a population, spatial module analysis identified two main modules with unique spatial distributions. Module 1 was in the outer cortex (genes: Cux2, Rorb, Rora; high expression, low DNA methylation). Module 2 was in the inner layer (genes: Sox5, Rbfox1, Bcl11a; high expression, low DNA methylation there). This emphasized Layer5a heterogeneity at the multi-omics level. Application to human myocardial infarction: We applied SIMO to datasets covering human myocardial infarction (scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, ST). Key cell types (cardiomyocytes, endothelial cells, fibroblasts, myeloid cells, vSMCs) were successfully mapped with similar spatial distributions across modalities. Cardiomyocyte subclusters (clusters 0,1,7) showed significant differences in distance to the infarct area. Cluster 7 was closest; pathway analysis linked it to wound healing and repair. Cluster 0 was related to normal heart function. Analysis of ATAC data for cardiomyocytes also showed groups adjacent to and distant from the infarct. Wound healing scores in adjacent regions were significantly higher, indicating activated accessibility of repair genes.

section: Application to Myocardial Infarction Part 2 and Discussion
text: By integrating gene expression with motif activity from ATAC, modularity analysis identified two gene-motif modules in cardiomyocytes with distinct spatial distributions: module 1 closer to infarct zone and myeloid cells, module 2 farther. A negative correlation existed between module 1 activity and distance to myeloid cells/infarct. GSEA showed module 1 involved in platelet function, calcium levels, and cytokine pathways, potentially crucial in myocardial infarction. Module 1 included transcription factors like ATF6 and IRF2 linked to myocardial injury. Analysis of fibroblasts also yielded two gene-motif modules. Module 1 was closer to myeloid cells; module 2 transcription factors (KLF15, OVOL2, RUNX1, SMAD3) may regulate cardiac fibrosis and repair. Transcription factors in module 1 (KLF2, ZEB1) are involved in inflammatory/immune responses. Discussion: SIMO uses optimal transport algorithms for sequential spatial mapping of single-cell data from various modalities, enabling integration of two or more omics types. Advantages: 1) Capable of reconstructing spatial distribution of multiple modalities; 2) Excels at handling modal data with opposing biological signals; 3) Possesses gene regulation and spatial regulation analysis capabilities; 4) Theoretically compatible with any omics data that can be linked to transcriptome features. Benchmarks showed SIMO's superior performance, especially for non-transcriptomic data. Applications in mouse cortex and human myocardial infarction revealed multimodal heterogeneity and proposed potential therapeutic targets. As single-cell omics evolves, SIMO could provide a more comprehensive landscape of gene regulatory networks.

section: Methods Part 1: SIMO Toolkit and Alignment
text: Methods: SIMO toolkit. Alignment of modality 1: Input scRNA-seq and ST data are processed. Top shared genes are used. Gene matrices are normalized/scaled. For scRNA-seq, data: $X\in R^{p\times n}$ (expression) and $E\in R^{h\times n}$ (reduced-dim rep). For ST: $Y\in R^{p\times m}$ (expression) and $Z\in R^{2\times m}$ (spatial coordinates). k-NN graphs are constructed for each dataset, resulting in distance matrices $G\in R^{n\times n}$ and $G'\in R^{m\times m}$. Input datasets as triplets $(X, G, w)$ and $(Y, G', w')$. Alignment matrix $\Pi=[\pi_{ij}]\in R_{+}^{n\times m}$. The objective function minimizes: $$F(\Pi; X, G, Y, G', d, \alpha)=(1-\alpha) C_{\exp}+\alpha C_{\text{graph}} \qquad(1)$$ where $\alpha\in[0,1]$, $C_{\exp}=\sum_{i,j} d(x_{.i}, y_{.j})\pi_{ij}$ (expression similarity), $C_{\text{graph}}=\sum_{i,j,k,l} (g_{ik}-g'_{jl})^2 \pi_{ij} \pi_{kl}$ (graph distance cost). Cell composition is determined via NNLS deconvolution. Coordinates assignment of modality 1: Cells initially placed at spot coordinates, then refined. For cell i in spot j with neighbors, coordinates: $$(x_i, y_i)=\left(x_j+\frac{\sum_{k=1}^n (x_k - x_j)p_k}{n}, y_j+\frac{\sum_{k=1}^n (y_k - y_j)p_k}{n}\right) \qquad(2)$$ where $p_k$ are scaled PCCs. A pseudo-spot is added if needed. Alignment of modality 2: Preprocessing and Leiden clustering. Gene activity scores link modalities. For clusters, average profiles $X'$ and $Y'$ are created, PCC matrix M: $M_{i,j}=PCC(x'_i, y'_j)$. After scaling, UOT solves: $$F(\Pi; M, \text{reg}_m, g, g') = \sum_{j,l} M_{jl}\pi_{jl} + \text{reg}_m \cdot \sum_j \pi_{.j}\log(\frac{\pi_{.j}}{g_j}) + \text{reg}_m \cdot \sum_l \pi_{.l}\log(\frac{\pi_{.l}}{g'_l}) \qquad(5)$$ For paired populations, GW transport calculates cell-cell pairing. Coordinates assignment of modality 2: Uses low-dimensional embedding and cosine similarity for correction.

section: Methods Part 2: Analysis Modules, Simulation, Evaluation, Data Availability
text: Gene regulation analysis: Converts modality 2 data to gene-feature matrix (e.g., motif activity). Calculates fold changes and PCCs between TF activity and gene expression. Spatial regulation analysis: Extracts expression and spatial info. Applies spatial smoothing and cross-modal smoothing. Calculates regulatory score (ratio feature2/feature1). Constructs spatial kernel matrix: $K(i,j)=exp(-d_{ij}/(2\sigma^2))$. Uses weighted correlation and CC to identify feature modules. Simulation data: Based on mouse cortex SNARE-seq/ISSAAC-seq. Created patterns with 3 or 10 cell types. Introduced pseudocount δ for noise. Biological data: Three benchmark datasets: 1) Mouse embryo Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, 2) Mouse brain Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, 3) Mouse brain Spatial CUT&Tag-RNA-seq (H3K27me3). Performance evaluation: Mapping accuracy $A=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\frac{n'_j}{n_j}$. RMSE: $RMSE=\sqrt{\frac{1}{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M}(\sum_{n=1}^{N}(y'_{n,j}-y_{n,j})^2)}$. JSD for 'spot': $JSD(P_j\|Q_j)=\frac{1}{2}D_{KL}(P_j\|M_j)+\frac{1}{2}D_{KL}(Q_j\|M_j)$ (9). JSD for 'type' defined similarly across cell types. Data and Code Availability: All relevant data are within the article and Supplementary Information. Source data provided. The SIMO toolkit is available on GitHub: https://github.com/ZJUFanLab/SIMO and Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14498257.
